video_title: Ted Oakley: Wall Street Is Running Investors Off A Cliff
gist: Veteran high-net-worth advisor Ted Oakley (Oxbow Advisors) frames the 2026 melt-up in semiconductors and the SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic IPO wave as classic late-cycle 'lemming' greed, urging capital preservation and treasury-heavy positioning while flagging gold below $4,000 and commodities (copper, energy, ags) as where he is buying. He argues the baby-boomer passive bid will reverse over the next decade and warns a generational bear market is approaching, likely in 2027.

insights.claim: The rush of mega-IPOs (SpaceX, with OpenAI and Anthropic expected to follow, plus new offerings from Google and Meta) is a classic late-bull-market greed signal that historically precedes market tops.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A surge in easy-to-sell IPOs marks the distribution phase; investors should treat heavy IPO supply as a sell/de-risk signal rather than an entry point.
insights.quote: It doesn't happen overnight, but those are the kinds of signs you look for toward the end because what happens is you just breed all this greed and there's a lot of greed out there right now.

insights.claim: 70% of the top 10 IPOs over the last 20 years traded lower one year after listing, per Oakley's own review.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Historical base rate argues against chasing hot IPOs in the aftermarket; buyers of SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic at offering prices face poor one-year odds.
insights.quote: I went back and looked at the top 10 IPOs in the last 20 years. And believe it or not, 70% of them were lower a year later.

insights.claim: Oakley would buy SpaceX as a business but considers the IPO price overpriced, valuing its Starlink satellite-internet revenue while doubting the 'space' segment, and is taking a wait-and-see approach.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Even a constructive long-term view on Starlink doesn't justify the IPO valuation; patient investors may get a better post-IPO entry.
insights.quote: I actually would love to own SpaceX, but I'm not comfortable at this IPO price.

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO was reportedly ~4x oversubscribed with the issuer effectively dictating the offering price to bankers, an abnormal sign of extreme demand and greed on Wall Street.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley / Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Issuers naming their own price to oversubscribed books signals peak speculative froth, a contrarian warning for broad market exposure.
insights.quote: Tell them what price it's going to come at.

insights.claim: Buffett's avoidance of IPOs on the stock side is a benchmark: if the best investor in the world never buys IPOs, retail buyers should question their edge in doing so.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Use Buffett's IPO abstention as a discipline check before participating in offering manias.
insights.quote: If the best investor in the world never bought an IPO, what makes you think that you could buy IPOs and make a lot of money?

insights.claim: Oakley expects the current selloff in AI/semiconductor names to extend another three to four weeks and deliver more downside than investors anticipate before a typical pre-election rally resumes.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Near-term traders should expect continued weakness into mid-summer rather than buying the first dip.
insights.quote: I'll be surprised if that selling doesn't go on another three or four weeks.

insights.claim: Second years of presidential terms tend to be low-return years that swoon into June-July-August before a feverish rally into the election, framing 2026 as a mid-year drawdown year.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Seasonality argues for buying mid-year weakness and trimming into the late-year election rally.
insights.quote: These second years of these presidential terms tend to be low return years a lot of times. But the key point to them is they swoon in the middle of the year.

insights.claim: Oakley does not expect a 30-50% crash in 2026 because there is no recession yet, but views 2027 as a 'totally different story' with elevated risk of a generational bear market.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Investors get a window in 2026 to de-risk; 2027 is flagged as the higher-probability period for a major washout.
insights.quote: Now next year, totally different story.

insights.claim: Oakley would treat any new S&P 500 high (potentially above 8,000) between now and January as a prime opportunity to take gains and de-risk, since 'everybody's in' at the top.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A final melt-up to new highs should be sold into rather than chased; sets a concrete de-risking trigger.
insights.quote: It wouldn't surprise me to make one another new high before you finish this thing up in the S&P. Maybe go up over 8,000 or something. But see, by that time... Everybody's in.

insights.claim: Government deficit spending plus AI hyperscaler capex plus bonus/accelerated depreciation under the 'big beautiful bill' create a money 'juggernaut' that makes a near-term recession unlikely.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host), endorsed by Oakley
insights.so_what: Macro bears should not position for an imminent recession while these stimulus flows persist; downside is more likely a 2027 event.
insights.quote: It's like just a juggernaut of money and it's it's hard to see the economy going into recession while those spending flows are going on at the magnitude that they currently are.

insights.claim: Oakley cites historical six-year sequences where the S&P gains 15-20% for five years then surrenders all of it in a single 50% sixth-year drop, illustrating that buy-and-hold can net zero across a full cycle.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Capital preservation matters more than peak gains because recovering from a 50% loss requires a 150% gain just to break even.
insights.quote: I can show you where you can make a direct fiveyear, five, 15 or 20% gain in the S&P and then in the sixth year have a 50% down and you give every bit of it up.

insights.claim: Oxbow refuses to own semiconductors and AI stocks directly, instead playing the AI narrative via cheap copper, energy and natural gas names that supply data centers.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A 'picks-and-shovels' commodity approach offers AI-buildout exposure at lower valuations than the chipmakers themselves.
insights.quote: If you're telling me you really believe the narrative on the AI buildout, then we're right in the middle of it because we own copper and we own energy and natural gas.

insights.claim: Oakley compares today's AI capex boom to the late-1990s fiber overbuild, where excess 'dark fiber' capacity wasn't recognized as unusable until ~2002, implying AI overcapacity may not be obvious for roughly two years.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: AI overcapacity could become apparent on a multi-year lag; the reckoning may arrive only when ROI on data-center spend proves low or negative.
insights.quote: It's very similar to fiber in the late 90s and early 2000s they built too much fiber but they didn't realize it that they couldn't use it all until about 2002.

insights.claim: Jesse Felder's physics-based thesis (relayed by Taggart) argues the AI data-center buildout must bust because there aren't enough workers, permitted land or copper to build at projected rates, with project delays and cancellations already appearing.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host), citing Jesse Felder
insights.so_what: Physical bottlenecks could force AI capex and analyst estimates lower within 3-6 months, a potential bear-market trigger to watch.
insights.quote: There might be enough chips to fill the data center, but we don't have the the workers to build them fast enough. We don't have the permitted land.

insights.claim: Five of the Magnificent Seven trade below their October/November 2025 levels, with Google the only meaningful gainer and Apple barely up, because non-spenders aren't participating in the AI capex rally.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Market leadership has narrowed sharply to AI-capex beneficiaries; the broad mega-cap complex has been dead money for 7-8 months despite holders refusing to sell gains.
insights.quote: Five out of the seven are below where they were in October of 25 right today.

insights.claim: Oxbow cut gold and miners way back and sold all silver in the first weeks of 2026 due to froth, and now plans to add gold, miners and a little silver back as gold approaches/falls below $4,000 (down from ~$5,500).
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A concrete re-entry trigger: gold below $4,000 is Oakley's buy zone after a ~27% correction; miners offer leveraged upside.
insights.quote: If you if you give us a a gold count below 4,000, I'm I feel quite certain you'll see us come back into a number of things.

insights.claim: Oxbow's top gold holding is Agnico Eagle, bought four years ago, which corrected ~35% from its high; the position was cut in half early in 2026 with plans to re-add.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Names a specific miner and entry discipline (trim into froth, re-add into 35% corrections) for precious-metals investors.
insights.quote: Our number one holding there is Agnico Eagle.

insights.claim: Oxbow booked a 211% silver gain in 2025 and exited because late-arriving momentum/meme buyers (people wiring $100k to buy silver at $90-100) signaled a blow-off top; silver has since fallen to ~$68.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Retail FOMO at $90-100 silver marked the top; the ~32% drop to $68 validates selling into vertical moves and waiting to re-enter.
insights.quote: We had a 211% gain in... 25 and it we knew that it was too frothy and everybody, you know, everybody was chasing then.

insights.claim: Oakley argues a strong 10-year commodity bull market is likely because nations are now hoarding oil, iron ore, copper and critical minerals rather than coordinating supply, structurally pushing prices higher.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Resource nationalism / hoarding is a structural tailwind for commodities; supports overweight positioning for the next decade.
insights.quote: Since all the countries hoard everything now relative to commodities the chances of us having a strong commodity market the next 10 years are pretty good because nobody's working within in tandem with anybody else.

insights.claim: Per Jeff Currie's view relayed by Oakley, US strategic reserve drawdowns have left oil storage tanks low enough that scarcity could become visible by July, with the SPR unlikely to ever be fully refilled.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors (citing Jeff Currie)
insights.so_what: Low physical oil inventories plus a depleted SPR set up a potential summer oil price catalyst.
insights.quote: The tanks are low now. In other words, you'll get down to the point where they don't have any oil. And so he was thinking by July maybe that's true.

insights.claim: Baby boomers own roughly half the US stock market (~$30 trillion of ~$60 trillion) via 401ks and investments, and Oakley argues they cannot stay fully invested for another 20 years as they age, get sick, and de-risk.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Demographic selling pressure threatens the passive bid; aligns with Mike Green's thesis that index inflows could reverse.
insights.quote: The baby boomers own half the market between their 401s and their average and their investments, they own 30 trillion.

insights.claim: In 10 years the average baby boomer will be ~82-83, near US life expectancy (~85 for a current 65-year-old), implying a large share will be deceased or forced to draw down portfolios within a decade.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Aging mechanics turn boomers from net buyers into net sellers, a structural headwind for passive equity flows.
insights.quote: 10 years from now, 10 years from right now, the average baby boomer will be 82 going on 83 years old.

insights.claim: Today's retirees and current generations (Gen X, millennials) hold a higher percentage of their portfolios in equities than any prior retired cohort, raising systemic fragility if markets fall.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart (host), endorsed by Oakley
insights.so_what: Record equity allocations mean any drawdown triggers heavier-than-usual forced de-risking and capital outflows.
insights.quote: Today's retirees have a higher percent of their portfolios in stock uh than ever I think than ever for any retired category.

insights.claim: Oakley draws a parallel to the 1969-1973/74 bust that 'stunned' the all-in silent generation for ~25 years, warning the over-allocated boomer cohort faces a similar fate this cycle.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Historical precedent suggests a coming bear could durably scar over-invested retirees, reinforcing the case for current de-risking.
insights.quote: They lost so much money in 73 and 74 and I mean a lot of money that they just they didn't want to look at another stock.

insights.claim: Oxbow holds roughly 40-45% in short-term treasuries and has avoided long treasuries for years, keeping dry powder to deploy into screened value names as the market corrects.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Concrete allocation benchmark: a near-half cash/T-bill stance positions for opportunistic buying, not full participation in the melt-up.
insights.quote: We have about 40 probably about 45% in short-term treasuries.

insights.claim: Oxbow buys commodity-adjacent equities like Archer Daniels Midland, Corteva, Union Pacific (awaiting the Norfolk Southern deal) as cheaper ways to express the economy/commodity theme.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Names specific ag and rail holdings as value alternatives to crowded AI trades; the UNP-NSC merger is a catalyst to monitor.
insights.quote: You want to own things that work in that direction like Archer Daniels Midland and Cortiva and companies like that.

insights.claim: Oxbow's core discipline is buying a dollar of value for 70-80 cents from a watchlist of a couple hundred companies, holding cash until names hit valuation screens rather than chasing momentum.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A Buffett-style margin-of-safety process; investors can mirror it by maintaining a watchlist and waiting for valuation triggers.
insights.quote: We try to take a dollar worth of value and buy it for 70 or 80 cents.

insights.claim: Oakley advises windfall recipients (e.g., SpaceX employees with option payouts, business sellers, heirs) to park nearly all proceeds in sub-one-year treasuries for 12-18 months before investing.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: For sudden-liquidity events near a market top, defensive T-bill parking avoids deploying into froth and preserves optionality.
insights.quote: Just ice it in treasuries 100% guaranteed, short-term, I'm talking about less than a year, and get it through this next 12 or 15 months, 18 months.

insights.claim: Oakley warns that Wall Street investment banks exist to sell products at the wrong time, not to serve the individual investor's long-term interest, echoing David Lighter's 'Stop Making Stupid Investments.'
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Treat sell-side recommendations skeptically, especially near tops when banks push the hottest products.
insights.quote: Most of what comes off the Wall Street investment banks is not going to be good for you... they're there to sell you something.

insights.claim: Oakley prioritizes advice from people who actively manage money and are accountable to clients over newsletter writers and strategists who 'just have an opinion.'
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Weight commentary by whether the speaker has capital and reputation at risk; discount pure opinion sources.
insights.quote: You take you give me somebody that manages money though and I'm going to listen to them... they're having to put the money where the mouth is every day.

insights.claim: Oakley advises seeking out advisors via trusted word-of-mouth rather than engaging those who solicit you, since charismatic salespeople are not necessarily good capital managers and large firms often charge unreasonable fees.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley / Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Selection heuristic: vet advisors on performance and fees, distrust inbound sales pitches, favor experience.
insights.quote: The kids that don't make it after first year or two is because they couldn't sell.

insights.claim: Oakley's central rule for windfall recipients is capital preservation first ('don't lose money'), arguing the families who keep wealth vividly remember how hard it was to earn while those who squander it forget.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Behavioral discipline (memory of effort, downside focus) predicts long-term wealth retention better than return-chasing.
insights.quote: The people that I think the people that squander the money, they've forgotten somewhere along the way how much time and effort and money and sacrifice it took to put together that much money.

insights.claim: Oakley warns that successful entrepreneurs often transfer their high-risk, leveraged, control-oriented business habits into public markets where they have no control, leading to oversized losing bets.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: Newly liquid founders should recognize markets are a different game; concentrated 'big bet' instincts destroy preserved capital.
insights.quote: They use some of the same things in making investment decisions they did in the business itself, which is it's okay to get aggressive and... get leveraged.

insights.claim: Oakley argues investors have forgotten that stocks carry risk, and his forthcoming book ('Asleep at the Wheel') will use graphs to show boomers with overweight 401k equity exposure what a major correction would do to them.
insights.speaker: Ted Oakley, Oxbow Advisors
insights.so_what: A complacency warning: passive boomer portfolios concentrated in the same indices are vulnerable, reinforcing the de-risking thesis.
insights.quote: People have forgotten that stocks have risk. They've forgotten that.
